predicted_value: The Contractor and Owner waive Claims against each other for consequential, special, indirect, exemplary, or punitive damages arising out of or relating to this Contract. question: What are the are the Liquidated Damages Terms & Conditions?
predicted_value: The Owner will suffer substantial hardship and incur substantial damages if the Work is not completed on time, or if the Contractor fails to achieve Substantial Completion on or before the scheduled Substantial Completion date set forth above, as such date may be modified under the Contract Documents. The parties agree that such damages are difficult or impossible to determine with certainty, and therefore have in good faith estimated as fair compensation (and not as a penalty) the liquidated damages set forth below. If the Contractor fails to achieve Substantial Completion on or before the scheduled date set forth above, as such date may be modified in accordance with the Contract Documents, the Contractor shall pay the Owner liquidated damages, as fair compensation (and not as a penalty), in accordance with the following: $2,000 per day for each day beyond the Substantial Completion date. The assessment of liquidated damages shall be paid per calendar day until Substantial Completion shall be achieved. Liquidated damages shall be assessed cumulatively in accordance with the Contract Documents. Contractor hereby expressly agrees to waive its right to challenge the enforceability of this provision, and agrees that the liquidated damages amounts represent a reasonable measurement of damages, and not as a penalty, that Owner will sustain in the event of late performance. These liquidated damages are in lieu of Owner’s actual direct or consequential damages or losses as a result of Contractor's failure to achieve Substantial Completion of the Work. The Owner may deduct liquidated damages from any amounts due or to become due Contractor, or the Owner may require Contractor to pay any liquidated damages which exceed amounts due or to become due to Contractor within ten (10) days after Owner’s written request. Contractor waives and Owner is not required to comply with any other notice provisions in order to assess and/or deduct liquidated damages. Owner may assess and/or deduct the amount of liquidated damages by withholding the amount of the liquidated damages from the payment due to Contractor on any pay application(s) and paying that amount to itself.
explanation: The predicted answer captures the key term of $2,000/day beyond the Substantial Completion date but includes additional details not present in the gold answer. There is no contradiction.

question: What are the Payment & Performance Bond Article Terms & Conditions?
gold_value: Performance and Labor and Materials Bond (AIA 312) equal to 100% of Contract Price and amended by change orders if approved by owner and at the time of Contract award remaining in full effect through warranty and extended warranty periods.  Amount paid as cost of work is limited to premium evidenced on an invoice from surety.
predicted_value: The Payment & Performance Bond Article Terms & Conditions are as follows: The Owner has the right to require the Contractor to furnish bonds that cover the Contractor’s faithful performance of the Contract and payment of obligations arising thereunder. Upon request of the Owner, the Contractor must purchase and maintain a Performance and Labor and Materials Bond executed by a Surety with a minimum rating of A.M. Best A, in favor of the Owner, on a standard AIA bond form, A312. This bond must be in an amount equal to 100% of the Contract Price and must remain in full force and effect through the warranty and extended warranty periods. The Contractor must promptly furnish a copy of the bonds to any potential beneficiary upon request. Any defaults or claims in excess of the limits of bonds provided or SDI purchased must be paid by the General Contractor without an increase to the GMP. The Contractor must pay all costs arising from a Subcontractor’s default if the limits are exhausted and must indemnify the Owner from all claims arising from such defaults.

The Contractor must provide evidence that the proposed substitution or deviation will deliver a quality result equal to the original specifications. If the Architect or Owner finds the evidence insufficient, they may reject the substitution or deviation. If approved, the Contractor is responsible for any additional work and materials required due to the deviation, at no cost to the Owner and without project delays, unless the Owner or Architect is the sole cause. Approval of a substitution does not relieve the Contractor from liability for non-compliance with the Contract Documents. The Architect is responsible for reviewing and incorporating Owner-requested substitutions into the design. The Contractor is also responsible for ensuring that all work complies with approved submittals and for informing the Architect in writing of any deviations at the time of submittal. The Contractor is not relieved of responsibility for errors or omissions in submittals by the Architect's approval.

question: What are the GMP Shared Savings Terms & Conditions?
gold_value: Sliding scale first $100,000 30% to Contractor reducing 5% each $100,000 thereafter up to $500,000 and Contractor's share capped at $100,000
predicted_value: To the extent that the Cost of the Work and the Contractor’s Fee is less than the Guaranteed Maximum Price, the Contractor shall be entitled to share such “savings” realized by the Owner in connection with the Project as follows: --first $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 30% to the Contractor and 70% to Owner; --second $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 25% to the Contractor and 75% to Owner; --third $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 20% to the Contractor and 80% to Owner; --fourth $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 15% to the Contractor and 85% to Owner; --fifth $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 10% to the Contractor and 90% to Owner. All remaining savings shall be to and for the benefit of the Owner. The Contractor’s share of the savings as broken down above shall be capped at $100,000.

question: What are the GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions?
predicted_value: The GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions are as follows: The Guaranteed Maximum Price (GMP) is the total maximum cost to be paid by the Owner for the Contractor’s full and complete performance under the Contract Documents, including all services, labor, materials, equipment, and fees. If the Cost of the Work and the Contractor’s Fee is less than the GMP, the Contractor is entitled to share the savings realized by the Owner. The distribution of savings is tiered: the first $100,000 of savings is distributed 30% to the Contractor and 70% to the Owner; the second $100,000 is distributed 25% to the Contractor and 75% to the Owner; the third $100,000 is distributed 20% to the Contractor and 80% to the Owner; the fourth $100,000 is distributed 15% to the Contractor and 85% to the Owner; and the fifth $100,000 is distributed 10% to the Contractor and 90% to the Owner. All remaining savings benefit the Owner. The Contractor’s share of the savings is capped at $100,000.

question: What are the Contingency Usage Terms and Conditions?
gold_value:
- cover costs reimbursable as a cost of the work
- not reasonably anticipated at time of Agreement
- refinement of drawing details
- corrective work not due to fault/negligence
- nonperformance by sub

predicted_value: The Contractor must submit a Contingency Use Authorization form for the Owner's review and approval before using any part of the Contractor's Contingency. The form must detail the amount and basis for the proposed use and include supporting documentation. The Contractor cannot use the Contingency for General Conditions costs or any amount exceeding the threshold without prior approval from the Owner. Unauthorized use of the Contingency must be returned to the Owner immediately. Monthly reports on the use of the Contingency are required, and the Owner has the discretion to reject any proposed or actual use that does not meet the Contract requirements. The Contingency can be used for costs arising from design refinements, corrective work not due to the Contractor's fault, nonperformance by Subcontractors, unanticipated costs during construction, and unintended gaps or overruns in the buy-out of the Work. It cannot be used for work due to the Contractor's negligence, the Contractor's Fee, costs recoverable from Subcontractors or insurance, or any item not constituting a Cost of the Work. Requests to use the Contingency for amounts over $35,000 or to increase the General Conditions line item require a Contingency Use Authorization form.
explanation: The predicted answer captures most of the important content from the gold answer and adds additional procedural details. There is no contradiction, but the additional details slightly reduce the semantic similarity.

question: What are the Schedule Requirements Terms & Conditions?
gold_value:
- Update Contract Schedule with each pay application
- Submittal schedule to be coordinated with Contract Schedule; if submittal schedule is not maintained Contractor may not claim delay due to submittal review
- Changes in Contract Schedule are only accepted via Change Order
- Contractor delays/recovery to schedule are reimbursable only within GMP

The Contractor must coordinate its work with the work of other third-party contractors. The Contract Schedule will be reviewed by the Owner and the Architect for compliance with the requirements of this Article and will be accepted by them only in the form of an approved Change Order. Schedules submitted or used by the Contractor that are not the current Contract Schedule accepted in an approved Change Order are for the sole use of the Contractor, at his risk, and do not relate to this Contract. Unless specifically required by law, no initial payment under this Contract shall be due until the Contract Schedule has been approved by the Owner and the Architect. If the Contractor and Owner have agreed that the Contractor should be permitted to extend the time for completion as provided in Subparagraph 4.3.7, the calendar dates in the Contract Schedule shall be adjusted accordingly to retain their same relationship to the adjusted date of Substantial Completion, and the dollar value of work to be completed as the first of each month shall be adjusted pro-rata. The Contractor shall furnish sufficient forces, plant and equipment and shall work such hours, including night shifts and lawful overtime operations as may be necessary to ensure the prosecution of the work in accordance with the Contract Schedule. If, in the opinion of the Owner, the contractor falls behind schedule, the Contractor must take such steps as may be necessary to improve his progress, and must submit his plans demonstrating the manner in which the desired rate of progress may be regained. It shall be the responsibility of the Contractor to maintain his schedule so as not to delay the progress of the Work or the scheduled work of other persons. If Contractor delays the progress of the Work, in order to regain the time lost and to maintain the Contract Schedule, it shall be the responsibility of the Contractor to increase the number of shifts, days of Work, and/or to the extent permitted by law, to institute or increase overtime operations, all without additional cost to the Owner in excess of the GMP.

predicted_value: The Schedule Delay / Time Issues Terms & Conditions are detailed in various sections of the contract. If the Owner issues a written order to proceed and approves the Contractor's claim, the Contract Sum and/or Contract Time will be adjusted by Change Order. The Contractor must proceed with the work without delay, even if the claim is approved or denied in part or in whole. Claims for additional time must be made in writing and include an estimate of the probable effect of delay on the progress of the Work. The Contractor is entitled to an extension of time for delays caused by the Owner, Architect/Engineer, other contractors, changes in the Work, or certain uncontrollable events like natural disasters or government restrictions. However, the Contractor waives any claim for damages due to delays and can only claim an extension of time. Adverse weather conditions must be documented and proven to be abnormal and impactful on the critical path schedule. No time extension claims are allowed for delays caused by the Architect unless specific conditions are met. The Contractor must prove that the delay affects the critical path and that the Project is behind schedule. The Contractor must also maintain the schedule and take necessary steps to improve progress if they fall behind, without additional cost to the Owner.

predicted_value: In order to expedite the completion of the Work, the Owner, in his sole discretion, may direct the Contractor to increase the number of shifts, days of work, and/or perform overtime operations or to take such other measures as he deems necessary to expedite construction. Such instruction shall be in writing and signed by the Owner. The Contractor, however, shall not be entitled to any additional costs unless the need for such direction was not attributable to the actions, omissions, delays or other fault of the Contractor. In such an event, the Owner shall pay the Contractor the additional cost as mutually agreed upon in writing. If the Contractor is behind schedule due to his own actions, omissions, delays or other fault on its part and the Owner directs the Contractor to work additional shifts, overtime, expedite deliveries, supplement its forces or purchase additional materials or equipment in order to bring his work up to schedule, all additional cost in excess of the GMP shall be borne by the Contractor.

question: What are the Substantial Completion Terms & Conditions?
gold_value:
- 462 days from Commencement Date
- all work sufficiently complete in accordance with Contract Documents as required to allow for licensing inspection so Owner can occupy and utilize the work for its intended use
- Certificate of Occupancy are issued
- all finishes complete exclusive of punchlist
- all closeouts are been provided to owner

predicted_value: Substantial Completion is the stage when all of the Work is sufficiently complete in accordance with the Contract Documents and all Work under this Contract required to license the building as indicated in the Contract Documents, is complete to allow for a licensing inspection by a licensing agency for that particular location so that the Owner can occupy and utilize the Work for its intended use. However, in no event will Substantial Completion be reached within the meaning of this Contract until: (1) all Work under this Contract is completed to allow for necessary licenses, (2) Certificates of Occupancy are issued by the appropriate governmental authorities provided such certificates are not withheld through no fault of Contractor, (3) all finishes, including but not limited to, wall coverings, paint, carpeting, flooring, appliances, lighting fixtures, plumbing fixtures, softscape, hardscape, and paving have been properly installed (exclusive of Punch List work), (4) all as-built drawings, Operations and Maintenance manuals and warranties have been provided to the Owner, and (5) the Architect has issued a Certificate of Substantial Completion. Issuance of a license is not a criteria for Substantial Completion.

question: What are the Payment Terms & Conditions for Retainage Reduction?
gold_value: 10% through 50% complete on a line item basis.  Once line item reaches 50% no additional retainage is held
predicted_value: The Owner shall withhold as retainage 10% of each approved progress payment through 50% completion, on a line item basis, of the Project. Once a line item reaches 50% completion no further retainage will be held on that line item. This retainage shall be withheld until Substantial Completion as defined in the Contract Documents. Upon Substantial Completion of the Work by the Contractor, the Contractor shall be paid a sum sufficient to increase the total payments to the Contractor to 100% of the Cost of the Work and Fees, but less an amount equal to 200% of the costs of the Work for incomplete Work or Punch List Work. However, in no event will the retainage be reduced lower than the amount required by the Lender in the Construction Loan Agreement. In the event of any inconsistency between this retainage clause and the Construction Loan Agreement, the Construction Loan Agreement shall govern.

question: What are the Terms and Conditions for Payment of Offsite Stored Material?
gold_value: If approved in advance by the Owner, payment may similarly be made for materials and equipment suitably stored off the site at a location agreed upon in writing. Payment for materials and equipment stored on or off the site shall be conditioned upon compliance by the Contractor with procedures satisfactory to the Owner to establish the Owner's title to such materials and equipment or otherwise protect the Owner's interest, and shall include the costs of applicable insurance, storage and transportation to the site for such materials and equipment stored off the site.  The Contractor shall be responsible for any loss or damage to unincorporated materials or equipment stored offsite not covered by insurance.

question: What are the Terms & Conditions for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment?
gold_value: If the Architect does not issue a Certificate for Payment for which the Owner and Contractor agree that an undisputed amount is due, through no fault of the Contractor, within forty-five (45) days after receipt of the Contractor's Application for Payment, or if the Owner does not pay the Contractor within seven (7) days after the date established in the Contract Documents the amount certified by the Architect, then the Contractor may, upon seven (7) additional days' written notice to the Owner and Architect, stop the Work until payment of the amount owing has been received.  The Owner shall compensate the Contractor for the reasonable costs of shutdown and start-up, but this provision and such compensation shall nonetheless not be deemed to conflict with the “No Damages for Delay” provision set forth in the Contract, but shall be read in conjunction therewith.  Notwithstanding the foregoing, if the Owner and Contractor have a payment dispute, the Contractor shall proceed with the Work without delay, subject to Contractor’s right to assert a claim in accordance herein.
predicted_value: If an undisputed amount is due to the Contractor and not paid by the Owner within forty-five (45) days after receipt of the Contractor's Application for Payment, or if the Owner does not pay the Contractor within seven (7) days after the date established in the Contract Documents the amount certified by the Architect, the Contractor may, upon seven (7) additional days' written notice to the Owner and Architect, stop the Work until payment of the amount owing has been received. However, the Contractor shall not stop performance in the event there is a bona fide dispute with the Owner regarding any of the reasons set forth. The performance of the Work shall proceed without delay, but subject to Contractor’s right to assert a claim.
explanation: The predicted answer captures the essential terms and conditions for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment. It includes the conditions under which the Contractor can stop work and the requirement to proceed with work in case of a dispute, similar to the gold answer. However, it omits the specific mention of compensation for shutdown and start-up costs.

question: What are the Final Payment Terms & Conditions?
gold_value: § 9.10.2 Neither final payment nor any remaining retained percentage shall become due until the Contractor submits to the Architect (1) an affidavit that required payrolls, bills for materials and equipment, and other indebtedness connected with the Work for which the Owner or the Owner's property might be responsible or encumbered (less amounts withheld by Owner) have been paid or otherwise satisfied, (2) a certificate evidencing that insurance required by the Contract Documents to remain in force after final payment is currently in effect and will not be canceled or allowed to expire until at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice has been given to the Owner, (3) a written statement that the Contractor knows of no substantial reason that the insurance will not be renewable to cover the period required by the Contract Documents, (4) consent of surety, if any, to final payment, (5),  the lien waiver and release forms required under the Contract Documents, executed by Contractor and any other necessary parties, and (6) a letter copied to the Owner, stating that all close out Operations and Maintenance manuals and warranties have been submitted per Contract Document requirements. This scope shall include all “Architectural” and “Mechanical, Plumbing and Electrical” operations and maintenance manuals and warranty information. If the Contractor fails to furnish such releases or waivers as noted in item (5) as the Owner reasonably requires to satisfy the Owner that there are no outstanding liens, including outstanding liens by Subcontractors at any tier level, the Owner may require the Contractor, as a condition of final payment, to furnish a bond satisfactory to the Owner and meeting the requirements set forth in § 9.3.5 above. If a Subcontractor refuses to furnish a release or waiver required by the Owner, the Contractor may furnish a bond satisfactory to the Owner to indemnify the Owner against such lien and such personal liability arising from such claim. If such lien remains unsatisfied after payments are made, the Contractor shall refund to the Owner all money that the Owner may be compelled to pay in discharging such lien, including all costs and reasonable attorneys' fees.  Contractor agrees that it will obtain the agreement of each of its Subcontractors and Suppliers in its contracts with such parties that its Subcontractors and Suppliers will execute the lien waivers and releases.
explanation: No answer was provided.

question: What is the Concealed / Unknown / Hazardous Conditions Article #?
explanation: The predicted answer '10.3' does not match the gold answer 'General Conditions 4.3.4'.

question: What are the Concealed / Unknown / Hazardous Conditions Terms & Conditions?
gold_value: § 4.3.4	Concealed or Unknown Conditions. If the Contractor encounters conditions at the site that are (1) subsurface or otherwise concealed physical conditions that differ materially from those indicated in the Contract Documents or (2) unknown physical conditions of an unusual nature, that differ materially from those ordinarily found to exist and generally recognized as inherent in construction activities of the character provided for in the Contract Documents, the Contractor shall promptly provide notice to the Owner and the Architect before conditions are disturbed and in no event later than twenty-one (21) days after first observance of the conditions. The Architect will promptly investigate such conditions and, if the Architect determines that they differ materially and cause an increase or decrease in the Contractor’s cost of, or time required for, performance of any part of the Work, will recommend an equitable adjustment in the Contract Sum or Contract Time, or both. If the Architect determines that the conditions at the site are not materially different from those indicated in the Contract Documents and that no change in the terms of the Contract is justified, the Architect shall promptly notify the Owner and Contractor in writing, stating the reasons. If either party disputes the Architect’s determination or recommendation, that party may proceed as provided in the Disputes provisions.
predicted_value: If reasonable precautions will be inadequate to prevent foreseeable bodily injury or death to persons resulting from a material or substance, including but not limited to asbestos or polychlorinated biphenyl (PCB), encountered on the site by the Contractor, the Contractor shall, upon recognizing the condition, immediately stop Work in the affected area and report the condition to the Owner and Architect in writing. The Contractor shall have no responsibility for the discovery, presence, handling, removal, disposal or exposure of persons to hazardous substances existing in any form at the site unless the Contractor or anyone for whom Contractor is liable introduces the hazardous substance to the site, or unless the Contract Documents require the abatement and removal of said hazardous substances. The Owner shall obtain the services of a licensed laboratory to verify the presence or absence of the material or substance reported by the Contractor and, in the event such material or substance is found to be present, to verify that it has been rendered harmless. Unless otherwise required by the Contract Documents, the Owner shall furnish in writing to the Contractor and Architect the names and qualifications of persons or entities who are to perform tests verifying the presence or absence of such material or substance or who are to perform the task of removal or safe containment of such material or substance. The Contractor and the Architect will promptly reply to the Owner in writing. To the fullest extent permitted by law, the Owner shall indemnify and hold harmless the Contractor, and its Subcontractors and employees from and against claims, damages, losses and expenses, including but not limited to attorneys' fees, arising out of or resulting from performance of the Work in the affected area if in fact the material or substance presents the risk of bodily injury or death and has not been rendered harmless, provided that such claim, damage, loss or expense is attributable to bodily injury, sickness, disease or death, or to injury to or destruction of tangible property (other than the Work itself) and provided that such damage, loss or expense is not due to the sole negligence of a party seeking indemnity. The Owner shall not be responsible for materials and substances brought to the site by the Contractor unless such materials or substances were required by the Contract Documents and were obtained, handled and delivered in accordance with the Contract Documents.
explanation: The predicted answer addresses hazardous conditions but focuses on hazardous materials like asbestos or PCB, which is different from the concealed or unknown conditions described in the gold answer. There is some overlap in the general topic of hazardous conditions, but the specific terms and conditions differ significantly.
